claim_boundary: This contract covers one unsigned UEFI proof application with bounded live PBC1, PSM1, PKELF1, PBART1, PBLIVE3, PKMAP2, and PBEXIT1 integration, not the complete PooleBoot loader.

The proof loads and relocates PooleKernel, loads six exact-profile PBART1 envelopes into distinct zero-padded firmware page ranges, validates the kernel's supervisor W^X mapping plan, and retains every loaded range plus four private table pages, an eight-page guarded stack, and a one-MiB read-only handoff allocation.

PSM1 selects and digest-binds the kernel and six profile artifacts; PBART1 additionally binds each non-kernel role, version, payload length, and payload digest, but all artifacts remain unsigned and untrusted.

The initial-system payload is a deterministic PINIT1 declaration bundle validated by an independent host oracle, and unsigned development activation is denied; PooleBoot still treats the inner bytes as opaque and PooleKernel does not activate them.

The recovery payload is a deterministic PREC1 policy bundle validated by an independent host oracle, and unsigned development activation is denied; PooleBoot still treats the inner bytes as opaque, authenticated mutable state does not exist, and PooleKernel grants no recovery authority.

The symbols payload is a deterministic PSYM1 public diagnostic index validated by an independent host oracle, and unsigned consumption is denied; PooleBoot treats it as opaque, PooleKernel performs no lookup, runtime addresses remain undisclosed, private debug data stays off media, and no authority is created.

The microcode payload is a deterministic PMCU1 package containing only synthetic never-apply bytes; exact target selection and reset-based known-good policy are validated by independent host and no_std oracles, while PooleBoot and PooleKernel enforce nothing and no privileged revision probe, vendor authentication, or CPU update occurs.

Immediately before ExitBootServices, PooleBoot captures a bounded final UEFI memory map, normalizes descriptor-stride-aware entries, and finalizes development-profile PBP1 bytes inside the retained handoff allocation.

PBEXIT1 permits at most four attempts, retries only EFI_INVALID_PARAMETER after obtaining a fresh map key, forbids unrelated firmware calls after the first attempt, and proves zero firmware calls after success.

PKMAP2 requires CR0.WP, processor NX support, and EFER.NXE; it rejects LA57, PCID, occupied target slots, malformed coverage, writable-executable leaves, large leaves, retained overlap, and guard-layout drift.

PooleBoot activates the candidate CR3 only for complete alias and framebuffer verification, restores the exact original CR3 before the final firmware sequence, then retains the candidate root for a later kernel-transfer phase.
Framebuffer first and last translations, leaf sizes, effective permissions, and PAT, PCD, and PWT bits remain equal during validation; this proves no drift, not a final PooleOS cache policy.

Both serial and debugcon reconstruct the exact post-exit PBP1 bytes and cross-bind all seven artifact roles and physical ranges, the retained root, guarded stack, handoff physical and virtual addresses, and loader-reserved final-map coverage.

After ExitBootServices succeeds, PooleBoot disables interrupts, checks that the retained PBP1 bytes are unchanged, emits direct diagnostics without firmware, and halts permanently before changing CR3, RSP, or calling PooleKernel.

The retained PBP1 is development-only and intentionally fails the kernel-entry profile because signatures are absent; no artifact semantics, initial-system execution, microcode application, transferable production handoff, or final kernel address space is claimed.
The proof does not enroll keys, enforce Secure Boot, perform measured boot, use TPM state, or create a signature.
The deterministic raw image is development media, not an ISO, installer, recovery image, or physical-media qualification.
The stop marker proves the instrumented QEMU/OVMF path reached the post-exit halt boundary; it does not prove target-firmware behavior, physical hardware, or kernel execution.
QEMU and OVMF remain exact non-promoting development inputs with the provenance gaps recorded by the Tier 0 lock.
The static framebuffer identity is not the final animated PooleGlass boot identity or an accessibility acceptance result.
No N5 exit gate, production readiness, signing, release, or physical hardware claim follows.

open_items: Ratify or replace the candidate PSM1 format before stable boot ABI promotion.
Define and implement signed manifest authentication, trust anchors, algorithm agility, and revocation policy.
Persist and atomically enforce the minimum secure version across update and rollback.
Define the final transfer-time CR3 activation, stack switch, register ABI, and explicit revocation plan.
Authenticate the loaded initial-system, recovery, policy, symbols, microcode, and firmware artifacts through a ratified trust policy.
Implement PINIT1 parsing and semantic enforcement in PooleBoot before initial-system declarations can cross the firmware boundary.
Implement PooleKernel activation, capability issuance, transactional startup, rollback, and lifecycle enforcement for qualified PINIT1 declarations.
Implement authenticated PREC1 state persistence, PooleBoot enforcement, handoff binding, and PooleKernel-mediated recovery without ambient authority.
Implement signed PSYM1 parsing and bounded capability-authorized diagnostic consumption in PooleBoot and PooleKernel without staging private debug data.

Authenticate PMCU1, validate real vendor containers and licenses, observe privileged per-processor revisions, and implement early PooleKernel apply and post-verify enforcement before any production payload is included or applied.
Define and implement each remaining firmware-manifest and policy artifact's semantics, parser hardening, capability boundary, and lifecycle policy before execution or application.
Transfer to PooleKernel and capture entry, panic, recovery, and reset evidence.
Add fault-injected live EFI_INVALID_PARAMETER retry evidence rather than lifecycle-model evidence alone.
Define how PooleKernel reclaims final-map scratch pools after consuming PBP1.
Qualify hostile firmware behavior, target firmware, physical hardware, and a second clean builder.
Complete Secure Boot, measured boot, TPM policy, signing, ISO, installer, and recovery gates.
